events.text: Refactor the pagination in the blog index. Right now `posts.slice(start, end)` is computed on every render and the cursor handling is duplicated across three pages. Extract a shared `paginate()` helper and migrate the three call sites. Keep URL shape identical - ?page=N.

blocks.text: Plan: locate the three duplicated cursor blocks, factor the shared math into `lib/pagination.ts`, migrate each site, run the typecheck + tests. I'll keep the URL contract - `?page=N`, 1-indexed, default 10 per page - so no template or sitemap changes.

All three share the same shape: read `?page=N` (default 1), compute `start = (page-1) * PER_PAGE`, slice. The only variation is the source array. Clean extraction.

Writing `lib/pagination.ts` with a single `paginate<T>(items: T[], page: number, perPage = 10)` returning `{ items, page, totalPages, hasPrev, hasNext }`. The `hasPrev`/`hasNext` flags collapse the duplicated nav-link logic in each template.

blocks.text: Type error - `ctx.query.page` is `string | string[] | undefined` in Next.js. I forgot to coerce. The other two pages already had `parseInt(ctx.query.page ?? '1', 10)` in their original code; I need to keep that. Let me add a small `parsePage()` to the helper so each call site doesn't repeat it.
Clean typecheck. Running the full test suite now to catch any rendering regressions.

Does the helper handle the case where someone hits ?page=0 or ?page=-3? Want to make sure that doesn't 500.

blocks.text: `parsePage()` clamps to `max(1, …)` and `paginate()` clamps to `min(totalPages, …)`. So `?page=0` becomes page 1, `?page=-3` becomes page 1, `?page=999` becomes the last page. The `clamps page below 1` and `clamps page above totalPages` tests cover both. No 500 - worst case the user sees the first or last page of results.

Committing on `refactor/pagination-helper`.

<session-state>
DID: Extracted shared `paginate()` + `parsePage()` into `lib/pagination.ts` with 8 unit tests, migrated three duplicated cursor blocks (index, tag, author), kept the ?page=N URL contract intact. Net: +159, -28 across five files.
INSIGHT: Next.js's `ctx.query` types are `string | string[] | undefined` - every place we parsed `?page` was silently re-implementing the coerce-and-clamp dance. Centralizing it removed three subtle off-by-ones the duplicated code had drifted into.
NEXT_STEP_USER: Open the PR on `refactor/pagination-helper` and double-check the rendered nav-link disabled states on the last page of /tag/ and /author/ - those templates didn't have explicit tests for the link state, only the slice.
</session-state>
